svg.text: LRU Eviction Architecture — Doubly-Linked List + Hash Map
O(1) get/put operations, access patterns, Redis approximation vs true LRU
True LRU Architecture — Hash Map + Doubly-Linked List
Hash map gives O(1) lookup; linked list gives O(1) reorder
get("A") Operation — Move Accessed Node to MRU End
Step 2: Unlink Node(A) from current position
Step 3: Insert Node(A) at HEAD (MRU end)
After get("A") — A moved to MRU
put("X", val) Operation — Insert at MRU, Evict from LRU if Full
Before put("X", val) — Cache at capacity (5)
Step 1: Check if "X" exists in hash map
Step 2: Not found --> cache is full, evict TAIL
Step 3: Remove "E" from TAIL + hash map
Step 4: Create Node("X"), insert at HEAD
After put("X", val) — E evicted, X at MRU
Redis Approximated LRU vs True LRU — Memory vs Accuracy Trade-off
Exact ordering: HEAD <--> A <--> B <--> C <--> D <--> E <--> TAIL
Not practical for millions of keys
in a distributed cache like Redis
Memory per entry: key + value + 2 pointers (16 bytes on 64-bit)
For 10M keys: ~160 MB just for pointers
Sample N keys (default 5), evict least recently used among sample
Higher N = better approximation, more CPU. Lower N = faster, less accurate.
Memory efficient for millions of keys
Good enough hit rate (N=10 ~95% of true LRU)
used key if not in sample
Memory per entry: 3 bytes (24-bit idle counter) vs 16 bytes (2 pointers)
For 10M keys: 30 MB vs 160 MB -- 5x savings
